title: Certification Levels and Responsibilities
narrationScript: This lesson is about the people side of ultrasonic testing. Who is allowed to do what, and why the system is built that way. Start with two words that get blurred all the time. Qualification and certification are not the same thing. Qualification is what you are, the combination of training, experience, knowledge, and physical ability that makes you competent to do a job. Certification is a piece of paper, the written testimony from a specific authority that you are qualified, with a defined scope and an expiration date. In most of American industry, that authority is not a government agency and not a society headquarters. It is your employer. Here is how the framework works. A document called SNT TC One A is a recommended practice. It does not command, it recommends. Each employer takes those recommendations and writes its own binding company document, called the written practice, which spells out, for every method and every level, the required hours of formal training, the required experience, the examinations and their passing grades, the vision requirements, and how often you must recertify. When a contract needs hard requirements instead of recommendations, a standard takes over, such as ANSI ASNT CP One Eighty Nine, or, in the aerospace world, NAS Four Ten. There are also central certification programs administered outside the employer. But the working rule for you is simple. The governing document is the one your employer or your contract invokes. Read it, because auditors will. Now the levels. A Level One technician performs specific calibrations, specific tests, and specific acceptance or rejection determinations, all according to written instructions, and records the results. Notice how often the word specific appears. A Level One executes a defined task whose settings and acceptance values were spelled out in advance by someone at a higher level. Inside that box, a certified Level One works independently. Taking thickness readings on a marked grid. Scanning to an approved instruction. Logging data and flagging anything the instruction defines as reportable. Outside that box, the Level One stops and escalates. Choosing the technique, deviating from the instruction, or deciding what an unexpected indication means, none of that is Level One territory. A trainee sits one step lower still. A trainee is working toward qualification, may assist and practice only under direct supervision, and may not independently perform, evaluate, or report any test. A Level Two is the working backbone of most programs. The Level Two sets up and calibrates the equipment, conducts the examination, and then does the thing that defines the level. The Level Two interprets and evaluates the results against the applicable code, standard, or specification. Deciding whether an echo is harmless weld root geometry or a root crack is a Level Two call. The Level Two is expected to know the scope and the limitations of the method, to guide and supervise trainees and Level One personnel, and to organize and report the results. Depending on the written practice, a Level Two may also prepare the written instructions that Level Ones work from. A Level Three carries the program itself. The Level Three establishes, reviews, and approves the techniques and procedures everyone else works under, interprets codes and specifications, designates which method and technique a job requires, and is responsible for the operation as a whole. The Level Three also trains and examines candidates for Level One and Level Two, and is expected to have a practical grounding in materials and fabrication technology, plus general familiarity with the other NDT methods, because choosing between ultrasonics, radiography, magnetic particle, and penetrant is itself a Level Three decision. So how do you climb? Formal training hours come first, then documented experience, then examinations. Typically a general exam on the method, a specific exam on your employer's procedures and products, and a hands on practical, with a composite passing grade around eighty percent in common practice. Add a near vision check, usually every year, because a technician who cannot resolve fine detail on a screen or a scale cannot do this work reliably. Certification then runs for a fixed period, commonly three to five years depending on the governing document, before you recertify. One last thing, and it matters. Working inside your level is an ethics issue as much as a technical one. Overstating your certification, signing results you are not qualified to evaluate, or padding experience hours can end a career, because the whole employer based system runs on documented, honest qualification. Know your lane, work it with excellence, and respect the boundary. It exists so that every judgment is made by someone qualified to make it.
imagePrompt: An experienced inspector in a fabrication shop watches closely as a younger technician scans a thick steel weld with an angle-beam ultrasonic probe, the flaw detector resting on the plate between them.

sections.heading: Qualification Is Not Certification
sections.body:
Two words get blurred constantly, and the difference matters. **Qualification** is the demonstrated combination of training, experience, knowledge, and physical ability (vision, in particular) needed to perform a job. **Certification** is the **written testimony** that a person is qualified — a document, issued by a specific authority, with a scope and an expiration.

In the dominant U.S. industrial model, that authority is **your employer**. A recommended practice — **SNT-TC-1A** — provides guidelines, and each employer turns them into a binding company document called the **written practice**, which defines for each method and level:

- required **formal training hours** and **experience** (UT typically demands more of both than most other methods)
- the **examinations** — general, specific, and practical — and passing grades, with a composite around 80% typical
- **near-vision and color-vision** requirements and recheck intervals (near-vision typically annually)
- the **recertification** interval — commonly every three to five years per the governing document

Where contracts demand requirements rather than recommendations, a standard such as **ANSI/ASNT CP-189** or, in aerospace, **NAS 410** governs instead, and central certification schemes administered outside the employer also exist. The working rule is the same everywhere: **the governing document is the one your employer or contract invokes — read it.**

sections.caption: The path from trainee to certified technician in an employer-based program, with the documents that govern each step.

sections.heading: Level I: Test to the Letter
sections.body:
An NDT **Level I** is qualified to perform **specific** calibrations, **specific** tests, and **specific** acceptance or rejection determinations — according to **written instructions** — and to **record** the results. The word 'specific' is doing real work in that sentence: a Level I executes a defined task with the technique, instrument settings, and acceptance values already spelled out in an instruction prepared by a higher level. Within that box, a certified Level I works without someone standing over them: taking grid thickness readings, scanning per an approved instruction, logging data, and flagging any reading or indication the instruction defines as reportable.

Outside the box, the Level I must stop and escalate. A Level I does **not** select the method or technique, does not deviate from the instruction, and does not interpret indications the instruction does not cover — that judgment belongs to Level II and above.

A **trainee** sits a step lower still: working toward qualification, a trainee may assist and practice only under the **direct supervision** of certified personnel, and may not independently conduct, evaluate, or report any test.

sections.heading: Level II: Interpret and Evaluate
sections.body:
The NDT **Level II** is the working backbone of most UT programs, qualified to:

- **Set up and calibrate** equipment for the job — selecting wedge, frequency, and screen ranges within the approved procedure.
- **Interpret and evaluate** results against applicable codes, standards, and specifications — the call that an echo is weld-root geometry rather than a root crack is a Level II decision.
- Be **thoroughly familiar with the scope and limitations** of the method — knowing when UT is the wrong tool or when a result needs verification by another technique.
- **Guide and supervise** trainees and Level I personnel, exercising assigned responsibility for their on-the-job training.
- **Organize and report** the results of examinations.

Depending on the written practice, a Level II may also prepare the written instructions a Level I works from. Interpretation is where the responsibility sharpens: when a Level II accepts a weld, that judgment carries the program's credibility. The real difference between Level I and Level II is not scanning skill — many Level I technicians scan beautifully — it is the **authority to decide what an indication is and whether the part stays in service**.

sections.caption: Duties stack with level: each tier keeps the abilities below it and adds authority and judgment.

sections.heading: Level III: The Method Authority
sections.body:
The NDT **Level III** carries responsibility for the program itself, qualified to:

- **Establish, review, and approve** techniques and procedures — the documents everyone else works under.
- **Interpret codes, standards, and specifications**, translating contract requirements into the company's NDT practice — including **designating the method and technique** for a particular examination.
- Carry overall **responsibility for the NDT operations** they oversee, backed by practical grounding in materials, fabrication, and product technology.
- **Train and examine** Level I and Level II candidates — most written practices make the Level III responsible for qualification examinations.
- Maintain **general familiarity with the other NDT methods**, because choosing between UT, RT, MT, and PT is itself a Level III call.

A Level III need not be the best scanner in the building — the role is engineering judgment, documentation, and teaching. In a typical shop the Level III writes and approves the UT procedure, the Level II runs jobs and makes accept/reject calls under it, and Level I technicians execute defined tasks within it. That chain, with each level working inside its scope, is exactly what a customer or auditor expects to find.

sections.caption: Typical division of duties in an employer-based program — the employer's written practice settles the details.
rows: Scan and record per a written instruction
Interpret and evaluate results to a code
Prepare written instructions for Level I use

sections.title: Your written practice governs
sections.body: Hour counts, exam grades, vision schedules, and recertification intervals in any textbook — including this one — are **typical values**, not your requirements. The binding numbers live in **your employer's written practice** (per SNT-TC-1A) or in the standard your contract invokes (CP-189, NAS 410, or an ISO 9712-style scheme). Before you assume, read the document. Auditors will.

sections.heading: On the Floor: Knowing Your Lane
sections.body:
Picture a corrosion survey. A certified UT Level I works a marked grid on a vessel shell per a written instruction: couple, read, record, move on. The instruction says readings below 6.0 mm must be flagged. At one location the gauge flickers between 4 and 9 mm and will not settle. Logging '9 mm' would be a serious error; deciding 'it is a lamination, not wall loss' would exceed Level I authority. The correct move is the professional one: mark the location, note the behavior, and **call the Level II**, who re-examines with an A-scan instrument and makes the interpretation.

Working inside your certification is an ethics issue as much as a technical one. Misrepresenting your certification level, signing results you did not perform or are not qualified to evaluate, and padding experience hours are the kinds of acts that end careers — the entire employer-based system runs on documented, honest qualification. Know what your level authorizes, do that work excellently, and treat the boundary as a feature: it is how the system guarantees every judgment is made by someone qualified to make it.

figures.title: Qualification path in an employer-based program
Responsibility ladder: Level I, II, and III

checkQuestions.question: In an SNT-TC-1A-based program, NDT certification is issued by:
options: ASNT headquarters, automatically, after any exam
The technician's employer, in accordance with its written practice
The instrument manufacturer
The customer's purchasing department
checkQuestions.explanation: SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice for employer-based programs: the employer writes a written practice and issues (and owns) the certification.

checkQuestions.question: A Level I performing a thickness survey per a written instruction encounters an indication the instruction does not address. The proper action is to:
options: Evaluate it personally against the construction code
Ignore it — only items listed in the instruction count
Record what was observed and report it to a Level II or III for interpretation
Reject the component immediately
checkQuestions.explanation: Interpretation beyond the written instruction exceeds Level I authority; the professional response is to document the observation and escalate to a higher level.

checkQuestions.question: Which duty most clearly distinguishes a Level II from a Level I?
options: Interpreting and evaluating indications against a code or specification
Recording test data on a report form
Performing calibrations described in a written instruction
Applying couplant correctly during scanning
checkQuestions.explanation: Both levels can execute and record tests, but the authority to interpret what an indication is and evaluate it against acceptance criteria begins at Level II.

checkQuestions.question: Establishing and approving the UT procedures a shop works under is the responsibility of the:
options: Level I
Level II
Trainee under supervision
Level III
checkQuestions.explanation: The Level III establishes, reviews, and approves techniques and procedures, interprets codes, and carries responsibility for the NDT program as a whole.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about a trainee is correct?
options: A trainee may independently perform tests but not report them
A trainee may evaluate results if a Level II reviews them later
A trainee works only under direct supervision and may not independently conduct, evaluate, or report tests
A trainee holds a provisional certification with reduced scope
checkQuestions.explanation: A trainee is working toward qualification and is not certified; all work is performed under the direct supervision of certified personnel and carries no independent authority.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic UT Course §1.5 (responsibilities of levels of certification)
SNT-TC-1A (recommended practice: employer written practice, qualification and certification framework)
ANSI/ASNT CP-189 and NAS 410 (certification standards, general framework)
